title: Infections in Patients with Cancer
authors: Brahm H. Segal, Juan C. Gea-Banacloche

items.title: Why the depth and duration of neutropenia drive infection risk
items.content:
Neutropenia is the single most important predisposition to infection in cancer patients.
The risk of infection is proportional to the degree of neutropenia once the absolute neutrophil count (ANC) becomes <1000/µL, and regimens causing prolonged neutropenia also deplete monocytes and lymphocytes and damage mucosa.
Prolonged severe neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/µL for ≥2 weeks) puts patients at risk for invasive aspergillosis and other molds, while the combination of neutropenia and mucositis (as after AML induction) predisposes to oral ulceration, neutropenic enterocolitis, perirectal infection, and bloodstream infection by gut flora (viridans streptococci, enterococci, Enterobacterales).
Longer courses of therapy are required in patients with persistent, severe immunocompromise.
keyPoints: Infection risk rises as ANC falls below 1000/µL
ANC <500/µL for ≥2 weeks → invasive aspergillosis and other molds
Neutropenia + mucositis → gut-flora bacteremia, typhlitis, perirectal infection; reactivation of oral HSV
items.reference: Chemotherapy-Induced Neutropenia: "The risk of infection is proportional to the degree of neutropenia once the absolute neutrophil count (ANC) becomes <1000/µL."

items.title: How neutropenic fever is defined and why empirical antibiotics start immediately
items.content:
Neutropenic fever (NF) is a medical emergency because signs of infection are blunted and infection can progress rapidly.
The standard definition is a single oral temperature ≥38.3°C, or ≥38.0°C sustained over 1 hour, with an ANC <500 cells/µL (or expected to fall below 500 within 48 h).
During neutropenia, localizing signs and symptoms may be subtle or absent and infections may progress very quickly, which mandates early initiation of empirical antibacterial therapy whenever infection is suspected — ideally within 1 hour of NF onset.
Similar management applies whenever infection is suspected in a neutropenic patient (e.g., unexplained pain, tenderness, or erythema) even without fever.
keyPoints: NF = temp ≥38.3°C once (or ≥38.0°C for 1 h) with ANC <500/µL (or expected to fall below 500 in 48 h)
Localizing signs are often subtle/absent; infection progresses fast
Start empirical antibiotics within ~1 hour; treat suspected infection even without fever
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "The standard definition of neutropenic fever (NF) is a single oral temperature ≥38.3°C or a temperature of ≥38.0°C sustained over 1 h, with an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of <500 cells/μL..."

items.title: How to manage the first episode of neutropenic fever
items.content:
First-episode NF management is well evidence-based.
After a swift history and exam (focused on portals of entry — catheter exit site, mouth, perianal area) and blood cultures, start empirical broad-spectrum monotherapy with antipseudomonal activity, ideally within 1 hour.
Best-studied monotherapy agents are cefepime, ceftazidime, imipenem, meropenem, and piperacillin-tazobactam; the choice depends on local resistance patterns.
In clinically unstable patients (hypotension, organ injury suggesting sepsis), broaden the regimen (e.g., vancomycin + meropenem ± an aminoglycoside, adding an echinocandin if candidiasis risk).
keyPoints: Blood cultures, then empirical antipseudomonal monotherapy within ~1 h
Monotherapy options: cefepime, ceftazidime, imipenem, meropenem, piperacillin-tazobactam
If unstable/septic: broaden (vancomycin + meropenem ± aminoglycoside ± echinocandin)
items.reference: First Neutropenic Fever: "empirical antibiotic therapy is initiated using a single agent (monotherapy) with broad-spectrum activity, including coverage of P. aeruginosa. Ideally, antibiotics should be given within 1 h of the onset of NF."

items.title: Why vancomycin is not part of the routine initial neutropenic-fever regimen
items.content:
Adding gram-positive coverage reflexively does more harm than good.
Gram-positive coverage with a glycopeptide (vancomycin) is not routinely part of the initial NF regimen; it is reserved for specific situations.
IDSA guidelines recommend empirical vancomycin for sepsis, soft-tissue infection, suspected catheter exit-site infection, pneumonia, severe mucositis (if ceftazidime is the backbone, given viridans streptococcal risk), or known MRSA/penicillin-resistant pneumococcus carriage.
Meta-analysis shows routine vancomycin in the initial regimen does not improve outcomes but increases nephrotoxicity.
keyPoints: Do NOT add vancomycin routinely to first-line NF therapy
Reserve it for sepsis, soft-tissue/catheter/pneumonia, severe mucositis, or MRSA/resistant-pneumococcus carriage
Routine vancomycin adds nephrotoxicity without better outcomes
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "the routine inclusion of vancomycin in the initial regimen is not associated with better outcomes but does result in more nephrotoxicity."

items.title: Why a persistent but stable fever should not by itself change the antibiotic regimen
items.content:
Persistent fever is a common but poor reason to escalate antibiotics.
If fever continues on empirical therapy without a diagnosis, guidelines recommend continuing the same antibiotic without addition or modification in the absence of clinical changes or new microbiologic data.
The regimen should change only for new clinical features (e.g., hypotension) or new microbiology (e.g., a positive culture) — not merely because the fever persists.
However, the longer NF persists on broad-spectrum antibiotics, the higher the likelihood of invasive fungal disease, prompting either empirical antifungal therapy after ~5 days of fever or a preemptive (test-driven) antifungal strategy.
keyPoints: Stable persistent fever alone is not a reason to broaden antibiotics
Change only for new clinical signs or new microbiologic data
Prolonged fever raises invasive fungal disease risk → empirical (day 5) or preemptive antifungal strategy
items.reference: Persistent Fever: "The antibacterial regimen should be modified only if new clinical features arise...if new microbiologic data become available...and not just because of persistent fever."

items.title: How empirical and preemptive antifungal strategies differ
items.content:
Two approaches address the rising fungal risk of persistent NF.
The empirical antifungal strategy (standard for decades) starts antifungals after ~5 days of persistent fever; caspofungin and liposomal amphotericin B are the best-studied empirical agents.
The preemptive strategy instead performs tests focused on diagnosing occult fungal infection (e.g., galactomannan, CT) and starts antifungals only if those tests support invasive fungal disease — yielding similar outcomes with less antifungal use.
A common modern approach is to give posaconazole prophylaxis to high-risk neutropenic patients (e.g., AML induction) and not change the antifungal based solely on persistent or recurrent fever.
keyPoints: Empirical: antifungals after ~5 days of fever (caspofungin or liposomal amphotericin B)
Preemptive: antifungals only if tests (galactomannan/CT) support fungal disease — less antifungal use
Posaconazole prophylaxis in high-risk neutropenia; don't change antifungal on fever alone
items.reference: Persistent Fever: "This more recent approach has been named preemptive antifungal strategy and seems to result in similar patient outcomes with less use of antifungals."

items.title: Why fever at neutrophil recovery is often not an infection
items.content:
Timing reframes the meaning of fever in the neutropenic course.
Recrudescent fever (fever returning after ≥48–96 h afebrile while neutropenia persists) most often does represent an identified infection, and the regimen should be empirically broadened with intensive diagnostics (CT, sometimes CT-PET).
In contrast, fever occurring at the time of neutrophil recovery may be a previously silent infection unmasked by returning neutrophils (analogous to immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome) but is frequently noninfectious, related to myeloid engraftment.
The recommended response to fever coinciding with resolving neutropenia is a thorough diagnostic evaluation rather than any specific empirical antimicrobial regimen.
keyPoints: Recrudescent fever (during persistent neutropenia) usually IS an infection → broaden + intensive workup
Fever at neutrophil recovery is often noninfectious (engraftment/IRIS-like)
At recovery: investigate thoroughly rather than reflexively escalating antimicrobials
items.reference: Recrudescent Fever and Fever at the Time of Neutrophil Recovery: "fever is frequently noninfectious at this time and is related to myeloid engraftment."

items.title: How to select neutropenic-fever patients for outpatient oral therapy
items.content:
A low-risk subset can be managed at home with oral antibiotics.
Outpatient therapy is considered for patients at lower risk — typically solid (not hematologic) tumors with a short expected neutropenia duration (≤7 days).
Criteria include clinical stability, no localizing symptoms/signs, ability to eat and drink, absence of significant comorbidity, normal renal and hepatic function, and easy hospital access with a caregiver at home.
Outpatient oral therapy in adults is usually a quinolone (levofloxacin or ciprofloxacin) plus amoxicillin-clavulanate — but only for patients who did NOT receive a quinolone as prophylaxis.
keyPoints: Low-risk: solid tumor, expected neutropenia ≤7 days, clinically stable, no localizing signs, good support
Regimen: quinolone + amoxicillin-clavulanate
Not appropriate if a quinolone was used as prophylaxis
items.reference: OUTPATIENT THERAPY FOR NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "such regimens are appropriate only for patients who have not received a quinolone as prophylaxis."

items.title: How to distinguish mucosal-barrier-injury bacteremia from a catheter infection
items.content:
The organism and clinical setting point to the source of bacteremia.
Bacteremia occurs in about 10% of NF episodes but carries much higher mortality, especially with gram-negative organisms; most cases arise from mucosal barrier injury (MBI) with translocation of gut commensals during neutropenia, or from a central line (CLABSI).
An intestinal organism without local catheter signs suggests MBI; a skin-flora organism such as coagulase-negative Staphylococcus is more likely a CLABSI (or a contaminant/colonizer).
When the same organism grows from both central and peripheral cultures, bacteremia is confirmed, and a central culture turning positive ≥2 h before the peripheral one suggests catheter-related bacteremia.
keyPoints: Bacteremia in ~10% of NF but high mortality (esp. gram-negative)
Gut organism, no catheter signs → mucosal barrier injury; skin flora → likely CLABSI/contaminant
Differential time-to-positivity (central ≥2 h earlier) suggests catheter-related bacteremia
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "NF is associated with bacteremia in only 10% of cases, but this subgroup of patients has much higher mortality than the general group of neutropenic patients with fever, particularly with gram-negative bacteremia."

items.title: Why some infected catheters must be removed promptly
items.content:
Catheter salvage is sometimes possible but not for certain pathogens.
For CLABSI, the preferred treatment is catheter removal, though salvage of a tunneled catheter/port may be attempted in hemodynamically stable patients whose cultures clear on appropriate antibiotics (with antibiotic lock therapy if feasible).
However, the likelihood of success is low when the catheter is colonized with Staphylococcus aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida species, so these catheters should be removed as soon as possible.
Endocarditis is rare in neutropenia, and routine echocardiography for every bacteremia is not recommended — but S. aureus bacteremia always requires an echocardiogram.
keyPoints: CLABSI: remove the catheter (salvage possible only if stable and cultures clear)
Always remove catheters infected with S. aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida
S. aureus bacteremia mandates echocardiography (rule out endocarditis)
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "The likelihood of success when a catheter is colonized with S. aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida species is low, and these catheters should be removed as soon as possible."

items.title: Why corticosteroids broaden infection risk and require PJP prophylaxis
items.content:
Steroids suppress immunity and mask the signs of infection.
High-dose corticosteroids inhibit multiple innate and adaptive immune components, increasing risk in proportion to dose and duration and in combination with other immunosuppressants — and they can blunt signs such as fever and abdominal tenderness.
Their inhibition of T-cell immunity raises the risk of HSV/VZV reactivation, mycobacteria, Nocardia, dimorphic and mucosal Candida infections, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP).
Prophylaxis against PJP with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (or an alternative if intolerant) is recommended in patients receiving the adult equivalent of prednisone ≥20 mg/day for at least 4 weeks.
keyPoints: Steroid infection risk scales with dose/duration; signs (fever, tenderness) may be masked
T-cell suppression → HSV/VZV, mycobacteria, Nocardia, fungi, and PJP
PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) if prednisone ≥20 mg/day for ≥4 weeks
items.reference: Corticosteroids: "Prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii with trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)...is recommended in patients receiving the adult equivalent of prednisone ≥20 mg per day for at least 4 weeks."

items.title: Why asplenic cancer patients need vaccination and a fever action plan
items.content:
Loss of splenic function leaves patients exposed to encapsulated organisms.
Asplenic patients (after splenectomy, splenic irradiation, or chronic GVHD) are principally at risk for overwhelming sepsis by encapsulated bacteria — Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Neisseria meningitidis — so they should be vaccinated against these before planned splenectomy.
Antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., penicillin) should be considered for the first 2 years after splenectomy, especially with active malignancy or immunosuppression, and an asplenic patient with fever should be started promptly on antibiotics active against S. pneumoniae (ceftriaxone, or levofloxacin/moxifloxacin), adding vancomycin where resistance is common.
Asplenic patients also have increased risk of babesiosis, malaria, Capnocytophaga canimorsus (animal bites/scratches), and Salmonella.
keyPoints: Asplenia → overwhelming sepsis from encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, Hib, meningococcus)
Vaccinate before splenectomy; consider penicillin prophylaxis for 2 years; treat fever promptly (ceftriaxone)
Also at risk: babesiosis, malaria, Capnocytophaga (dog bites), Salmonella
items.reference: Splenectomy: "Asplenic patients are principally at risk for overwhelming sepsis by encapsulated bacteria like Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Neisseria meningitidis."

items.title: Why hepatitis B screening is mandatory before B-cell-depleting therapy
items.content:
B-cell depletion impairs antibody responses and can reactivate latent viruses.
B-cell-depleting agents (rituximab and other antibodies cause prolonged depletion; BTK inhibitors such as ibrutinib interrupt B-cell activation more transiently) increase risk of encapsulated bacteria and respiratory viral infections and blunt responses to vaccination (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, hepatitis B).
All patients who will receive B-cell-depleting agents should be screened for hepatitis B and given antiviral therapy (e.g., entecavir or tenofovir alafenamide) for active or prior infection, because of reactivation risk.
Rituximab is also associated with progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) and PJP, and BTK inhibitors can increase the risk of invasive aspergillosis, especially with concurrent corticosteroids.
keyPoints: Screen for hepatitis B and give antivirals before B-cell-depleting therapy (reactivation risk)
Increased encapsulated-bacteria/respiratory-viral risk; poor vaccine responses; severe COVID-19
Rituximab → PML/PJP; BTK inhibitors → invasive aspergillosis (worse with steroids)
items.reference: B Cell-Depleting Agents: "All patients who will receive B cell-depleting agents should be screened for hepatitis B infection and receive therapy for hepatitis B...for active or prior hepatitis B infection."

items.title: How CAR-T and bispecific antibody therapy create prolonged infection risk
items.content:
Newer immunotherapies cause deep, lasting immune deficits plus inflammatory toxicities treated with more immunosuppression.
CAR-T therapy uses lymphodepletion chemotherapy (usually fludarabine + cyclophosphamide), causing pancytopenia, and the persistent CD19-directed CAR-T cells produce by-design global B-cell depletion that can last for years.
Both bispecific antibodies (e.g., blinatumomab, CD3×CD19) and CAR-T can cause cytokine release syndrome (and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity), which is treated with high-dose corticosteroids and IL-6 blockade — further increasing infection risk.
Post-CAR-T prophylaxis (by extrapolation/expert opinion) includes levofloxacin during neutropenia, TMP-SMX while on steroids or with CD4 <200/µL, acyclovir, and mold-active prophylaxis during prolonged neutropenia.
keyPoints: CAR-T: lymphodepletion → prolonged pancytopenia; CD19 CAR-T causes years-long B-cell depletion
CRS treated with steroids + IL-6 blockade adds further immunosuppression
Prophylaxis: levofloxacin (neutropenia), TMP-SMX (steroids/CD4<200), acyclovir, mold-active agent
items.reference: Adoptive Cellular Therapy: "the duration of global B cell depletion can be for years. ACT can also be complicated by CRS and an immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome..."

items.title: How the cancer itself predisposes to specific infections
items.content:
The malignancy creates infection risk independent of therapy.
In solid tumors, obstruction is the key mechanism: airway-obstructing lung tumors cause post-obstructive pneumonia, obstructive pancreatic/biliary tumors cause cholangitis, and obstructive uropathy causes urinary tract infection; colon cancer is associated with Streptococcus gallolyticus and Clostridium septicum bloodstream infection.
Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia impair immunoglobulin production, causing recurrent sinopulmonary infections and poor vaccine responses.
T-cell leukemias/lymphomas (sometimes HTLV-1-associated) cause T-cell impairment with opportunistic infections by Pneumocystis jirovecii, Cryptococcus neoformans, tuberculosis, or disseminated strongyloidiasis; hairy cell leukemia is associated with nontuberculous mycobacterial infection.
keyPoints: Solid tumors: obstruction → post-obstructive pneumonia, cholangitis, UTI; colon cancer → S. gallolyticus / C. septicum
Myeloma & CLL: hypogammaglobulinemia → recurrent sinopulmonary infection, poor vaccine response
T-cell malignancies → PJP/Cryptococcus/TB/strongyloides; hairy cell leukemia → NTM
items.reference: RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER: "Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia are associated with impaired immunoglobulin production that can manifest with recurrent sinopulmonary infections and poor antibody responses..."

items.title: How antimicrobial prophylaxis is targeted to prolonged neutropenia
items.content:
Prophylaxis is reserved for the highest-risk patients and tailored to the deficit.
A fluoroquinolone such as levofloxacin should be considered in adults with prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/µL for ≥7 days); studies show fewer neutropenic-fever episodes and documented infections, and meta-analyses suggest a survival benefit.
In AML induction, posaconazole prophylaxis reduces invasive fungal disease and confers a survival benefit, so it is frequently recommended in profound, prolonged neutropenia.
Most patients with solid tumors require no antimicrobial prophylaxis, and antibiotics carry costs — C. difficile, resistance selection, and even diminished checkpoint-inhibitor efficacy through microbiome disruption.
keyPoints: Levofloxacin prophylaxis for prolonged neutropenia (ANC <500 for ≥7 days) — fewer infections, possible survival benefit
Posaconazole prophylaxis in AML induction reduces invasive fungal disease and improves survival
Solid tumors usually need no prophylaxis; antibiotics risk C. difficile, resistance, reduced checkpoint-inhibitor efficacy
items.reference: Chemotherapy-Induced Neutropenia: "prophylaxis with a fluoroquinolone like levofloxacin should be considered in adults with prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/μL for ≥7 days)."

items.title: How to evaluate pulmonary infiltrates in the neutropenic host
items.content:
Lung infiltrates in immunocompromised patients have a broad infectious and noninfectious differential.
Noninfectious causes include heart failure/fluid overload, transfusion reactions (TACO, TRALI), chemotherapy pneumonitis, organizing pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, and tumor spread; radiologic pattern helps — focal/unilateral suggests bacterial or mold infection, while multifocal/diffuse adds viruses, atypical bacteria, and PJP.
Early bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage is recommended (highest yield for diffuse/multifocal processes), and the empirical regimen should combine an antipseudomonal β-lactam with vancomycin and an anti-Legionella agent (azithromycin, fluoroquinolone, or doxycycline).
Single or multiple dense, well-circumscribed nodules (especially >2 cm), with halo or reverse-halo signs, suggest invasive fungal disease in high-risk patients.
keyPoints: Broad differential: include heart failure, TACO/TRALI, drug pneumonitis, DAH, tumor
Early BAL (best for diffuse/multifocal); empirical antipseudomonal β-lactam + vancomycin + anti-Legionella
Dense nodules >2 cm ± halo sign → invasive fungal disease in high-risk hosts
items.reference: Pneumonia: "Early bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) in neutropenic patients with pneumonia is recommended. The yield of BAL is highest for diffuse and multifocal processes."

items.title: Why invasive fungal sinusitis is a surgical emergency
items.content:
Angioinvasive sinus fungal infection can kill quickly without surgery.
Invasive fungal sinusitis in neutropenia may be caused by Aspergillus, agents of mucormycosis, Fusarium, and dematiaceous molds; CT clues include marked asymmetry, heterogeneous sinus contents, and later bony erosion, and endoscopy may show pale, devitalized mucosa or a necrotic turbinate from the angioinvasive process.
Invasive fungal sinusitis during neutropenia mandates surgical resection — repeated trips to the operating room plus optimal antifungal therapy are necessary to save the patient's life.
Because mucormycosis and fusariosis are possible, many experts recommend a lipid formulation of amphotericin B (the antifungal of choice for mucormycosis) together with posaconazole.
keyPoints: Causes: Aspergillus, mucormycosis agents, Fusarium, dematiaceous molds (angioinvasive)
Mandates surgical resection (repeated debridement) plus antifungals to save the patient
Empirical antifungals often lipid amphotericin B (covers mucormycosis) ± posaconazole
items.reference: Sinusitis: "Invasive fungal sinusitis during neutropenia mandates surgical resection. Repeated visits to the operating room in conjunction with optimal antifungal therapy are necessary to save the patient's life."

items.title: How neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis) is recognized and managed
items.content:
Gut wall invasion during neutropenia produces a characteristic syndrome.
Neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis) presents with fever, abdominal pain and tenderness, and diarrhea (occasionally bloody), most often involving the cecum; CT typically shows a right-lower-quadrant inflammatory mass with thickened bowel wall and fat stranding, and blood cultures are often positive with bowel flora.
Management is usually conservative — broad-spectrum antibiotics (carbapenem or piperacillin-tazobactam; add metronidazole if cefepime/ceftazidime is used), bowel rest, decompression, and nutritional support — with surgery reserved for perforation, peritonitis, or hemodynamic instability.
C. difficile is also more common in cancer patients (healthcare/antibiotic/chemotherapy exposure); test every hospitalized neutropenic patient with diarrhea, and treat first-line with fidaxomicin (oral vancomycin as alternative).
keyPoints: Typhlitis: fever + RLQ pain + diarrhea; CT shows thickened cecal wall — manage conservatively, surgery only for perforation/instability
Use broad-spectrum antibiotics (carbapenem/pip-tazo; add metronidazole with cefepime/ceftazidime)
Test all neutropenic diarrhea for C. difficile; first-line fidaxomicin
items.reference: Neutropenic Enterocolitis (Typhlitis): "Conservative management with broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest, bowel decompression, and nutritional support is usually preferred. Surgery may be required if the patient deteriorates..."

items.title: How to prevent infections in high-risk cancer patients
items.content:
Prevention is layered and tailored to immune status.
For patients with prolonged neutropenia or transplant/cellular therapy, leukemia and transplant wards use HEPA filtration, frequent air exchanges, and positive-pressure rooms primarily to reduce mold-spore exposure; houseplants are restricted, and construction requires stringent infection-control precautions.
Low-microbial diets are NOT supported by evidence and harm nutrition/quality of life; instead, patients should follow tailored CDC food-and-water safety advice (hand hygiene, washing produce, avoiding raw/undercooked meat/fish and unpasteurized dairy).
Patients should avoid gardening/mulching (spore exposure), farm/exotic animals and animal waste (e.g., litter boxes), and acquiring new pets while immunocompromised, and limit exposure during community respiratory-virus outbreaks (masking, avoiding crowds).
keyPoints: HEPA/positive-pressure rooms and no houseplants/gardening reduce mold exposure
Avoid low-microbial diets (no benefit, harms nutrition); use CDC food/water safety guidance
Avoid animal waste, new/exotic pets, and crowded indoor settings during viral outbreaks
items.reference: PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS: "The old practice of implementing \"low-microbial diets\" for neutropenic patients did not show convincing benefit in well-designed studies and carries the disadvantages of limiting nutrition and affecting quality of life."

items.question: Which set of values meets the standard definition of neutropenic fever?
items.explanation: Neutropenic fever is a single oral temperature ≥38.3°C (or ≥38.0°C sustained over 1 h) with an ANC <500/µL, or an ANC expected to fall below 500 within 48 h.
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "a single oral temperature ≥38.3°C or a temperature of ≥38.0°C sustained over 1 h, with an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of <500 cells/μL..."

items.question: A clinically stable patient develops neutropenic fever with no localizing source. What is the most appropriate initial empirical therapy?
options: Antipseudomonal monotherapy (e.g., cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) within 1 hour
Await blood culture results before any antibiotic
items.explanation: Stable NF of unclear etiology is treated with empirical broad-spectrum monotherapy with antipseudomonal activity (cefepime, ceftazidime, a carbapenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam), ideally within 1 hour of onset. Waiting for cultures is unsafe given rapid progression.
items.reference: First Neutropenic Fever: "empirical antibiotic therapy is initiated using a single agent (monotherapy) with broad-spectrum activity, including coverage of P. aeruginosa."

items.question: Empirical vancomycin should be added to the initial neutropenic-fever regimen in which situation?
options: Isolated stable fever with no localizing signs
Suspected catheter exit-site infection or known MRSA carriage
items.explanation: Vancomycin is not routine; it is reserved for sepsis, soft-tissue or catheter exit-site infection, pneumonia, severe mucositis (if ceftazidime is the backbone), or known MRSA/penicillin-resistant pneumococcus carriage. Routine use adds nephrotoxicity without benefit.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "the addition of a glycopeptide is reserved for clinical situations in which a gram-positive pathogen resistant to the standard regimen...is more likely."

items.question: A neutropenic patient remains febrile but clinically stable on day 3 of appropriate empirical antibiotics, with no new findings or cultures. What is recommended?
options: Add vancomycin because the fever persists
Continue the same antibiotic regimen without modification
Switch to a different β-lactam empirically
items.explanation: Persistent stable fever alone is not a reason to change the regimen; modify only for new clinical features or new microbiologic data. Prolonged fever does, however, raise the consideration of invasive fungal disease.
items.reference: Persistent Fever: "guidelines recommend continuing the same antibiotic without addition or modification in the absence of clinical changes or new microbiologic data."

items.question: Which agents are the best-studied for empirical antifungal therapy of persistent neutropenic fever?
items.explanation: Caspofungin and liposomal amphotericin B are the best-studied empirical antifungal treatments for persistent neutropenic fever, started around day 5 of fever in the empirical strategy.
items.reference: Persistent Fever: "Caspofungin and liposomal amphotericin B are the best-studied empirical antifungal treatment of NF..."

items.question: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis with TMP-SMX is recommended for patients receiving approximately:
options: Prednisone ≥20 mg/day (adult equivalent) for at least 4 weeks
items.explanation: PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX (or an alternative) is recommended for patients receiving the adult equivalent of prednisone ≥20 mg/day for at least 4 weeks, reflecting steroid-induced T-cell suppression.
items.reference: Corticosteroids: "recommended in patients receiving the adult equivalent of prednisone ≥20 mg per day for at least 4 weeks."

items.question: An asplenic cancer patient develops fever at home. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Observe for 24 hours before any antibiotic
Start prompt antibiotics active against S. pneumoniae (e.g., ceftriaxone)
Reassure and recheck temperature in a week
items.explanation: Asplenic patients are at risk for overwhelming sepsis from encapsulated organisms; fever requires prompt antibiotics active against S. pneumoniae (ceftriaxone, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone), adding vancomycin where resistance is common.
items.reference: Splenectomy: "Asplenic patients with fever should be started promptly on antibiotics active against S. pneumoniae (e.g., ceftriaxone or levofloxacin or moxifloxacin)."

items.question: Before starting rituximab, which screening test is essential to prevent a serious reactivation?
items.explanation: All patients receiving B-cell-depleting agents should be screened for hepatitis B and given antiviral therapy for active or prior infection, because of the risk of hepatitis B reactivation.
items.reference: B Cell-Depleting Agents: "All patients who will receive B cell-depleting agents should be screened for hepatitis B infection and receive therapy for hepatitis B..."

items.question: A neutropenic patient with a central line grows an intestinal (gut-flora) organism in blood with no local catheter signs. This bacteremia is most likely due to:
options: Mucosal barrier injury with bacterial translocation
items.explanation: An intestinal organism without local catheter signs is considered related to mucosal barrier injury (MBI) with translocation of gut commensals; skin-flora organisms (e.g., coagulase-negative staph) are more suggestive of CLABSI or contamination.
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "If the isolate is an intestinal bacterium, it is considered related to MBI in the absence of local signs of infection involving the venous catheter."

items.question: Which catheter-colonizing pathogen most strongly indicates that the central catheter should be removed promptly?
options: A single bottle growing coagulase-negative Staphylococcus after 36 h
items.explanation: Catheter salvage is unlikely to succeed when the catheter is colonized with S. aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida species; these catheters should be removed as soon as possible.
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "The likelihood of success when a catheter is colonized with S. aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida species is low, and these catheters should be removed as soon as possible."

items.question: In which situation is echocardiography specifically required during a neutropenic bacteremia?
items.explanation: Routine echocardiography for every neutropenic bacteremia is not recommended, but patients with S. aureus bacteremia do require an echocardiogram to evaluate for endocarditis.
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "Routine echocardiogram in every case of bacteremia in neutropenic patients is not recommended, but patients with S. aureus bacteremia do require one."

items.question: Which patient is the best candidate for outpatient oral therapy of neutropenic fever?
options: An AML patient with expected 3-week neutropenia
A stable solid-tumor patient with expected neutropenia ≤7 days, tolerating oral intake, with good home support
A patient who received levofloxacin prophylaxis
items.explanation: Outpatient therapy suits low-risk patients: solid tumors, short expected neutropenia (≤7 days), clinical stability, no localizing signs, ability to eat/drink, and good access/support. Oral quinolone + amoxicillin-clavulanate is used only if a quinolone was not given as prophylaxis.
items.reference: OUTPATIENT THERAPY FOR NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "These patients typically have solid rather than hematologic tumors with a short expected duration of neutropenia, usually 7 days or less."

items.question: A neutropenic AML patient develops fever, right-lower-quadrant pain, and bloody diarrhea; CT shows a thickened cecal wall with fat stranding. What is the preferred initial management?
options: Conservative management with broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest, and decompression
items.explanation: This is neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis). Management is usually conservative — broad-spectrum antibiotics (carbapenem or piperacillin-tazobactam; add metronidazole if cefepime/ceftazidime used), bowel rest, decompression, nutritional support — with surgery reserved for perforation, peritonitis, or instability.
items.reference: Neutropenic Enterocolitis (Typhlitis): "Conservative management with broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest, bowel decompression, and nutritional support is usually preferred."

items.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for Clostridioides difficile-associated disease in a cancer patient?
items.explanation: First-line treatment of C. difficile-associated disease is fidaxomicin, with oral vancomycin as an alternative; for ileus or overwhelming disease, oral fidaxomicin/vancomycin is combined with IV metronidazole.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile-Associated Disease: "First-line treatment is fidaxomicin, with oral vancomycin as an alternative."

items.question: A neutropenic patient develops a tender erythematous papule that becomes necrotic and ulcerated (ecthyma gangrenosum). Which organism is most classically responsible?
items.explanation: Ecthyma gangrenosum (a tender papule becoming necrotic/ulcerated) is typically caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa (by local inoculation or hematogenous seeding), though other gram-negatives or molds like Fusarium can cause it. Focal skin lesions in neutropenic patients should be biopsied.
items.reference: SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS: "Ecthyma gangrenosum appears as a tender, erythematous papule that then becomes necrotic and ulcerated...typically of P. aeruginosa..."

items.question: Listeria monocytogenes meningitis in an immunocompromised cancer patient is best treated empirically with:
items.explanation: Listeria monocytogenes (most common with impaired cellular immunity) is treated with ampicillin plus gentamicin; TMP-SMX is used in penicillin allergy. Note that standard meningitis cephalosporins do not cover Listeria.
items.reference: CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS: "The combination of ampicillin and gentamicin is recommended. In cases of penicillin allergy TMP-SMX may be used."

items.question: Why should echinocandins (e.g., caspofungin) not be relied upon to treat CNS fungal infections?
options: They are inactive against all fungi
They do not achieve therapeutic levels in the brain or cerebrospinal fluid
items.explanation: Echinocandins do not achieve therapeutic levels in the brain or CSF, so they should not be used for CNS infections. Voriconazole is preferred for CNS aspergillosis and liposomal amphotericin B for CNS mucormycosis.
items.reference: CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS: "Echinocandins do not achieve therapeutic levels in the brain or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), and they should not be relied upon for the treatment of CNS infections."

items.question: Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis (e.g., levofloxacin) is appropriate to consider in which scenario?
options: All patients with solid tumors on hormonal therapy
Adults with prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/µL for ≥7 days)
Any patient receiving a single cycle of chemotherapy
items.explanation: Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis should be considered in adults with prolonged neutropenia (ANC <500/µL for ≥7 days), where it reduces neutropenic-fever episodes and documented infections, with a possible survival benefit. Most solid-tumor patients need no prophylaxis.
items.reference: Chemotherapy-Induced Neutropenia: "prophylaxis with a fluoroquinolone like levofloxacin should be considered in adults with prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/μL for ≥7 days)."

items.question: What is the current recommendation regarding 'low-microbial diets' for neutropenic patients?
options: They are strongly recommended for all neutropenic patients
They lack convincing benefit and are replaced by tailored food-safety guidance
They eliminate the need for antibiotics
items.explanation: Low-microbial diets did not show convincing benefit and harm nutrition and quality of life; patients should instead follow tailored CDC food-and-water safety precautions (hand hygiene, washing produce, avoiding raw/undercooked foods and unpasteurized dairy).
items.reference: PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS: "The old practice of implementing \"low-microbial diets\" for neutropenic patients did not show convincing benefit..."

items.question: Streptococcus gallolyticus (bovis) or Clostridium septicum bloodstream infection should prompt evaluation for which underlying malignancy?
items.explanation: Bloodstream infection with Streptococcus gallolyticus or Clostridium septicum is associated with colon cancer, reflecting direct invasion through the colonic mucosa with enteric flora.
items.reference: RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER: "bloodstream infection (e.g., bloodstream infection by Streptococcus gallolyticus or Clostridium septicum in colon cancer)."

items.statement: Prolonged severe neutropenia (ANC <500/µL for ≥2 weeks) places patients at risk for invasive aspergillosis and other molds.
items.explanation: True — patients with more prolonged neutropenia (ANC <500/µL for ≥2 weeks) are at risk for invasive aspergillosis and other molds; refractory/relapsed leukemia further increases this risk.
items.reference: Chemotherapy-Induced Neutropenia: "Patients with more prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/μL for ≥2 weeks) are at risk for invasive aspergillosis and other molds."

items.statement: An infection is microbiologically or clinically documented in about 40% of neutropenic-fever episodes by standard methods.
items.explanation: True — using standard diagnostics, an infection is documented in approximately 40% of NF episodes; newer plasma cell-free DNA PCR identifies a potential bacterial etiology most of the time.
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "an infection is documented in approximately 40% of episodes of NF."

items.statement: Because localizing signs are blunted in neutropenia, empirical antibiotics should be started even when infection is suspected without fever (e.g., unexplained pain or erythema).
items.explanation: True — fever is the most common but not the only sign; similar empirical management is used whenever infection is suspected in a neutropenic patient, even without fever.
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "similar management should be used whenever infection is suspected in a neutropenic patient, such as in the presence of otherwise unexplained pain, tenderness, or erythema..."

items.statement: Bacteremia is documented in about 10% of neutropenic-fever episodes and carries higher mortality than NF overall.
items.explanation: True — NF is associated with bacteremia in only ~10% of cases, but this subgroup has much higher mortality, particularly with gram-negative bacteremia.
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "NF is associated with bacteremia in only 10% of cases, but this subgroup of patients has much higher mortality..."

items.statement: Corticosteroids can decrease the signs of infection such as fever and abdominal tenderness.
items.explanation: True — corticosteroids can blunt signs of infection (fever, abdominal tenderness), which can delay recognition; they also increase risk of viral, mycobacterial, Nocardia, fungal, and PJP infections.
items.reference: Corticosteroids: "Corticosteroids can also decrease signs of infection such as fever and abdominal tenderness."

items.statement: Asplenic patients have increased risk of babesiosis and of Capnocytophaga canimorsus infection associated with animal bites or scratches.
items.explanation: True — besides encapsulated bacterial sepsis, asplenic individuals have increased risk of babesiosis, malaria, Capnocytophaga canimorsus (animal bites/scratches), and Salmonella.
items.reference: Splenectomy: "Asplenic individuals also have increased risk of babesiosis, malaria, and infection with Capnocytophaga canimorsus (associated with animal bites or scratches) and Salmonella spp."

items.statement: Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia impair immunoglobulin production, causing recurrent sinopulmonary infections and poor vaccine responses.
items.explanation: True — both impair immunoglobulin production, manifesting as recurrent sinopulmonary infections and poor antibody responses to prior infections and vaccination.
items.reference: RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER: "Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia are associated with impaired immunoglobulin production that can manifest with recurrent sinopulmonary infections..."

items.statement: Invasive fungal sinusitis during neutropenia can be managed with antifungal therapy alone, without surgery.
items.explanation: False — invasive fungal sinusitis during neutropenia mandates surgical resection; repeated trips to the operating room plus optimal antifungal therapy are necessary to save the patient's life.
items.reference: Sinusitis: "Invasive fungal sinusitis during neutropenia mandates surgical resection."

items.statement: Voriconazole is the preferred agent for CNS aspergillosis, and liposomal amphotericin B for CNS mucormycosis.
items.explanation: True — voriconazole is preferred for CNS aspergillosis and liposomal amphotericin B for mucormycosis; echinocandins do not penetrate the CNS and should not be relied upon.
items.reference: CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS: "Voriconazole is the preferred agent for CNS aspergillosis and liposomal amphotericin B for mucormycosis."

items.statement: Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors can increase the risk of invasive aspergillosis, particularly when combined with corticosteroids.
items.explanation: True — BTK inhibitors have off-target effects on innate immune cells and can increase the risk of invasive aspergillosis, especially with concurrent corticosteroids.
items.reference: B Cell-Depleting Agents: "BTK inhibitors can have off-target effects on innate immune cells and increase the risk of other infections, including invasive aspergillosis, particularly when combined with other immunosuppressive agents, such as corticosteroids."

items.statement: Antibiotics have no effect on the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors.
items.explanation: False — there is precedent for antibiotics diminishing the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors by disrupting the microbiome, which is one reason to avoid unnecessary prophylaxis.
items.reference: ANTIMICROBIAL PROPHYLAXIS: "there is precedent for antibiotics diminishing the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors by disruption of the microbiome."

items.statement: Houseplants and gardening/mulching are safe activities for patients with prolonged neutropenia.
items.explanation: False — houseplants are restricted on leukemia/transplant wards and patients should avoid gardening, mulching, or similar activities during significant immune compromise, because of mold-spore exposure.
items.reference: PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS: "Patients should also not be involved in gardening, mulching, or similar activities that result in spore exposure."

items.assertion: Empirical antibiotics should be started promptly when a neutropenic patient is suspected of infection.
items.reason: During neutropenia, localizing signs may be subtle or absent and infections may progress very quickly.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because signs are blunted and infection progresses rapidly in neutropenia, prompt empirical therapy is mandated.
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "These two basic features mandate early initiation of empirical antibacterial agents in neutropenic patients whenever infection is suspected."

items.assertion: Vancomycin should be added to the initial regimen for every patient with neutropenic fever.
items.reason: Routine inclusion of vancomycin in the initial regimen improves survival in neutropenic fever.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Vancomycin is reserved for specific indications, and meta-analysis shows routine vancomycin does not improve outcomes but increases nephrotoxicity. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER: "the routine inclusion of vancomycin in the initial regimen is not associated with better outcomes but does result in more nephrotoxicity."

items.assertion: A stable persistent fever on appropriate antibiotics should not by itself prompt a change in the antibacterial regimen.
items.reason: The regimen should be modified only for new clinical features or new microbiologic data.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because changes should be driven by new clinical or microbiologic data, persistent stable fever alone does not justify modifying antibiotics.
items.reference: Persistent Fever: "The antibacterial regimen should be modified only if new clinical features arise...if new microbiologic data become available...and not just because of persistent fever."

items.assertion: A central catheter colonized with Candida should be removed as soon as possible.
items.reason: Catheter salvage with antibiotic/antifungal therapy has a low likelihood of success with Candida (as with S. aureus or mycobacteria).
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because salvage rarely succeeds with Candida, S. aureus, or mycobacteria, such catheters should be removed promptly.
items.reference: BACTEREMIA: "The likelihood of success when a catheter is colonized with S. aureus, mycobacteria, or Candida species is low, and these catheters should be removed as soon as possible."

items.assertion: Asplenic patients should be vaccinated against encapsulated bacteria before a planned splenectomy.
items.reason: Asplenic patients are principally at risk for overwhelming sepsis by encapsulated bacteria such as S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and N. meningitidis.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because the spleen is essential for clearing encapsulated organisms, vaccination before splenectomy mitigates the high sepsis risk.
items.reference: Splenectomy: "Patients should be vaccinated against these pathogens prior to planned splenectomy."

items.assertion: Hepatitis B antiviral therapy should be given to patients with prior hepatitis B who are starting a B-cell-depleting agent.
items.reason: B-cell depletion increases the risk of hepatitis B reactivation.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because B-cell-depleting agents can reactivate hepatitis B, screening and antiviral prophylaxis are required for active or prior infection.
items.reference: B Cell-Depleting Agents: "receive therapy for hepatitis B (e.g., with entecavir or tenofovir alafenamide) for active or prior hepatitis B infection."

items.assertion: Echinocandins should not be used as monotherapy for CNS fungal infections.
items.reason: Echinocandins do not achieve therapeutic levels in the brain or cerebrospinal fluid.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: poor CNS/CSF penetration makes echinocandins unreliable for CNS fungal infection, so agents like voriconazole or liposomal amphotericin B are used.
items.reference: CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS: "Echinocandins do not achieve therapeutic levels in the brain or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), and they should not be relied upon for the treatment of CNS infections."

items.assertion: A standard third-generation cephalosporin alone is adequate empirical therapy for suspected Listeria meningitis.
items.reason: Listeria monocytogenes is best treated with ampicillin plus gentamicin.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — standard meningitis cephalosporins do not cover Listeria, so ampicillin must be added. The reason is true and explains why the assertion fails. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS: "The combination of ampicillin and gentamicin is recommended."

items.assertion: Strict low-microbial 'neutropenic diets' should be prescribed for all neutropenic patients.
items.reason: Low-microbial diets have been shown in well-designed studies to substantially reduce infection.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Low-microbial diets did not show convincing benefit and harm nutrition/quality of life; tailored food-safety guidance is preferred. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS: "low-microbial diets\" for neutropenic patients did not show convincing benefit in well-designed studies..."

items.assertion: Fever occurring exactly as neutropenia resolves does not necessarily indicate a new infection.
items.reason: Such fever is frequently noninfectious and related to myeloid engraftment.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because fever at neutrophil recovery is often an engraftment phenomenon (or unmasking of a silent infection), it warrants thorough evaluation rather than reflexive antimicrobial escalation.
items.reference: Recrudescent Fever and Fever at the Time of Neutrophil Recovery: "fever is frequently noninfectious at this time and is related to myeloid engraftment."

items.subtopic: Infections in Patients with Cancer | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.title: Importance of Preventing and Managing Infections in Cancer Patients
items.content: Preventing, diagnosing, and treating infections is crucial for increasing survival rates and improving the quality of life in cancer patients. The infectious diseases consultant collaborates with various stakeholders to prevent infections and manage them effectively when they occur.
keyPoints: Infection drives excess hospitalization and mortality in cancer patients.
Prevention includes compliance with infection control guidelines, limiting pathogen exposure, vaccination, and targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis.
Diagnostic challenges include overlapping symptoms of infectious and noninfectious disorders.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · Infections in Patients with Cancer | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: Infections in Patients with Cancer | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.title: Approach to Treating Infections in Immunocompromised Cancer Patients
items.content: Treatment plans for infections in cancer patients should consider factors such as evidence of infection clearance, presence of a persistent nidus, specific pathogen, and patient's immune status. Longer treatment courses are often necessary for those with severe immunocompromise.
keyPoints: Longer antibiotic courses required for patients with prolonged neutropenia or severe immunocompromise.
Empirical therapy may be needed when invasive diagnostics are unsafe due to conditions like thrombocytopenia.
Individualized management tailored to patient's overall care goals, sometimes using antibiotics palliatively.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · Infections in Patients with Cancer | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER
items.title: Obstruction-related Infections in Solid Tumors
items.content: Solid tumors can cause obstructions leading to infections. For example, lung tumors may obstruct airways causing postobstructive pneumonia, while pancreatic and biliary tumors can lead to cholangitis. Obstructive uropathy from tumors predisposes to urinary tract infections.
keyPoints: Lung tumors can lead to postobstructive pneumonia due to airway obstruction.
Obstructive pancreatic and biliary tumors increase risk of cholangitis.
Tumors causing obstructive uropathy predispose to urinary tract infections.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER
items.title: Immune Deficits in Hematologic Malignancies and Associated Infections
items.content: Certain hematologic malignancies are linked with specific immune deficits. Acute leukemia and myelodysplastic syndrome often present with pancytopenia, leading to a lack of normal circulating neutrophils. GATA2 deficiency is associated with major infections and hematological issues. Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia impair immunoglobulin production, causing recurrent sinopulmonary infections.
keyPoints: Acute leukemia and myelodysplastic syndrome can present with pancytopenia at diagnosis.
GATA2 deficiency is linked to major viral, bacterial, and fungal infections.
Multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia are associated with impaired immunoglobulin production.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | RISK FACTORS INTRINSIC TO THE CANCER

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Neutropenia on Infection Risk in Cancer Patients
items.content: Neutropenia, particularly when prolonged and severe, significantly increases the risk of infections in cancer patients. The susceptibility to infection is directly proportional to the degree of neutropenia, with an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) below 1000/μL being a critical threshold.
keyPoints: Neutropenia drives susceptibility to infections in cancer patients.
Risk of infection increases as ANC falls below 1000/μL.
Prolonged neutropenia (e.g., ANC <500/μL for ≥2 weeks) raises the risk of invasive fungal diseases like aspergillosis.
Prophylactic antimicrobial therapy, such as fluoroquinolones or posaconazole, can reduce infection frequency and improve survival in patients with profound neutropenia.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Role of Chemotherapy-Induced Mucosal Injury in Infection Risk
items.content: Chemotherapy can cause mucosal injury, leading to increased infection risk. This is due to both pancytopenia and direct damage to the mucosal lining, which serves as a primary defense against pathogens.
keyPoints: Standard nonselective cytotoxic agents cause pancytopenia and mucosal disruption.
Mucositis predisposes patients to gastrointestinal infections, including neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis) and bloodstream infections by gut flora.
Reactivation of oral herpes simplex virus is common in leukemia therapy due to mucosal injury.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Influence of Corticosteroids on Infection Risk and Immune Function
items.content: Corticosteroids, used in various cancer therapies, can modulate immune function and influence infection risk. While they control inflammatory complications, their immunosuppressive effects may increase susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: Corticosteroids are part of regimens for hematologic malignancies and brain tumors.
They treat inflammatory complications like pneumonitis from immune checkpoint inhibitors.
Immunosuppression by corticosteroids can elevate infection risk.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Infection Risk and Prophylaxis in Patients on High-Dose Corticosteroids
items.content: High-dose corticosteroids suppress both innate and adaptive immunity, increasing infection risk. This is particularly relevant for patients undergoing induction therapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia, where the combination of cytotoxic agents and corticosteroids heightens this risk. Corticosteroid use can mask typical signs of infections such as fever.
keyPoints: Corticosteroids increase infection risk by suppressing T cell immunity.
Increased susceptibility to viral (e.g., HSV, VZV), mycobacterial, Nocardia, and fungal infections including PJP.
Prophylaxis with TMP-SMX is recommended for patients on prednisone ≥20 mg/day for at least 4 weeks.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Infection Risks and Management in Asplenic Patients
items.content: Splenectomy, whether for diagnostic or therapeutic reasons, impairs immune functions critical for fighting encapsulated bacteria. This increases the risk of overwhelming sepsis from pathogens like Streptococcus pneumoniae.
keyPoints: Asplenic patients are at high risk for infections by S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and N. meningitidis.
Antibiotic prophylaxis with penicillin is advised for the first 2 years post-splenectomy.
In areas with high resistance, vancomycin should be added to treatment regimens.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Increased Infection Risk and Vaccination Considerations in Patients on B Cell-Depleting Agents
items.content: B cell-depleting agents, such as rituximab and BTK inhibitors, increase the risk of infections due to their impact on immune function. These patients have diminished responses to vaccinations and are at higher risk for severe COVID-19.
keyPoints: Patients on B cell-depleting agents face increased risks from encapsulated bacteria and respiratory viruses.
Vaccination response is reduced, necessitating hepatitis B screening and treatment before therapy.
BTK inhibitors may increase the risk of invasive aspergillosis when combined with other immunosuppressants.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 3)
items.title: Understanding Complications of Adoptive Cellular Therapy
items.content: Adoptive cellular therapy (ACT), particularly CAR T cell therapy, involves lymphodepletion chemotherapy leading to prolonged pancytopenia. This can result in complications such as CRS and neurotoxicity, requiring intensive treatment with corticosteroids and IL-6 blockade.
keyPoints: Lymphodepletion regimens (e.g., fludarabine and cyclophosphamide) cause prolonged pancytopenia.
CRS and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome can be life-threatening.
Treatment often involves high-dose corticosteroids and IL-6 blockade.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 3)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 3)
items.title: Prophylaxis Strategies Post-ACT
items.content: Post-adoptive cellular therapy prophylaxis is guided by expert opinion, including levofloxacin during neutropenia and TMP-SMX when systemic corticosteroids are used or CD4 count <200/μL. Tailoring prophylaxis to the degree of immunosuppression is crucial.
keyPoints: Levofloxacin recommended during neutropenia post-ACT.
TMP-SMX advised with systemic corticosteroids or CD4 <200/μL.
Prophylaxis should be tailored based on individual immunosuppression levels.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RISK FACTORS ASSOCIATED WITH CANCER THERAPY (part 3)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH NEUTROPENIA | NEUTROPENIC FEVER
items.title: Early Empirical Antibacterial Therapy in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: Neutropenic fever (NF) is a critical condition requiring prompt empirical antibacterial therapy due to the rapid progression of infections and often subtle or absent localizing signs. NF is defined by specific temperature criteria and neutrophil counts, necessitating immediate treatment when infection is suspected.
keyPoints: Neutropenic fever is defined as a single oral temperature ≥38.3°C or sustained ≥38.0°C over 1 hour with an ANC <500 cells/μL, or expected to drop below this threshold within the next 48 hours.
Infections during neutropenia can progress quickly and may lack localizing signs, making early empirical antibacterial therapy essential when infection is suspected.
Fever is a common sign of infection in neutropenic patients, but other symptoms like unexplained pain or erythema should also prompt similar management.
Standard diagnostic methods identify an infection in approximately 40% of NF episodes.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH NEUTROPENIA | NEUTROPENIC FEVER

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH NEUTROPENIA | NEUTROPENIC FEVER
items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Advances in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: Diagnosing infections during neutropenia is challenging due to the lack of specific signs, with only a minority of cases having microbiologically documented infections. Newer diagnostic methods like plasma cell-free DNA PCR improve detection but often identify normal flora bacteria.
keyPoints: Infections are documented in about 40% of NF episodes using standard diagnostics such as cultures and imaging.
Plasma cell-free DNA PCR identifies potential bacterial etiologies more frequently, though these are often normal skin or bowel flora.
The majority of infections during neutropenia are caused by the patient's own microbiome due to disrupted physical barriers from cancer treatments.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH NEUTROPENIA | NEUTROPENIC FEVER

items.title: Initiation of Empirical Antibiotic Therapy in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: Empirical antibiotic therapy should be initiated within 1 hour of the onset of neutropenic fever using a broad-spectrum monotherapy that covers Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The choice of antibiotics, such as ceftazidime, cefepime, imipenem, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam, depends on local resistance patterns.
keyPoints: Empirical antibiotic therapy should be initiated within 1 hour of onset.
Monotherapy with broad-spectrum activity covering P. aeruginosa is recommended.
Choice of antibiotics varies based on local resistant bacteria trends.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Criteria for Antibiotic De-escalation in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: Antibiotics may be de-escalated or discontinued if specific criteria are met, including clinical stability and resolution of fever within 24–72 hours. This strategy aims to reduce antibiotic exposure and the risk of resistant pathogen selection.
keyPoints: De-escalation is considered safe with clinical stability and resolved fever in 24–72 hours.
Benefits include reduced antibiotic exposure and lower resistance risk.
Large trials are needed to evaluate safety and benefits.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Management of Persistent Fever in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: If fever persists without a diagnosis during empirical antibiotic therapy, continue the same regimen unless new clinical features or microbiologic data arise. Empirical antifungal treatment may be considered after 5 days of persistent fever.
keyPoints: Continue current antibiotics if no new clinical changes or microbiologic data are present.
Modify regimen only with new clinical features or positive cultures.
Consider empirical antifungal therapy after 5 days of persistent fever.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Preemptive Antifungal Strategy in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: A preemptive antifungal strategy can be employed for neutropenic fever, potentially reducing the use of antifungals while maintaining similar patient outcomes. The choice of empirical antifungal depends on prior prophylaxis; caspofungin and liposomal amphotericin B are well-studied options.
keyPoints: Preemptive antifungal strategy may reduce antifungal use with similar outcomes.
Empirical antifungal choice varies based on previous prophylaxis.
Caspofungin and liposomal amphotericin B are best-studied empirical treatments.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Management of Recrudescent Fever in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: Recrudescent fever, defined as the reappearance of fever after a period of being afebrile for 48–96 hours during persistent neutropenia, often indicates an infection. The approach involves modifying the antimicrobial regimen to cover resistant bacteria and fungi, with intensive diagnostic procedures like CT or CT-PET imaging recommended.
keyPoints: Recrudescent fever occurs after being afebrile for 48–96 hours during persistent neutropenia.
Infections are identified in most cases of recrudescent fever.
Modify antimicrobial regimen to cover resistant bacteria and fungi.
Intensive diagnostics, including CT or CT-PET imaging, should be undertaken.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF NEUTROPENIC FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Criteria for Outpatient Therapy in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: Outpatient therapy is suitable for neutropenic patients at lower risk of infectious complications. This includes those with solid tumors and a short expected duration of neutropenia (≤7 days). Candidates must be clinically stable, without localizing infection signs or symptoms, able to eat and drink easily, free from significant comorbidities like chronic lung or cardiovascular disease, have normal renal and liver function, and have easy access to hospital care and home support.
keyPoints: Patients with solid tumors are more suitable for outpatient therapy than those with hematologic malignancies.
Expected duration of neutropenia should be 7 days or less.
Clinical stability is required without localizing symptoms or signs of infection.
Ability to eat and drink without difficulty is necessary.
Absence of significant comorbidities such as chronic lung or cardiovascular disease.
Normal renal and liver function must be confirmed.
Easy access to a hospital and caregiver at home is essential.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · OUTPATIENT THERAPY FOR NEUTROPENIC FEVER

items.title: Antibiotic Regimen for Outpatient Therapy in Neutropenic Fever
items.content: For outpatient therapy, adults typically receive a combination of oral antibiotics: a quinolone (e.g., levofloxacin or ciprofloxacin) plus amoxicillin-clavulanate. This regimen is appropriate only if the patient has not previously received a quinolone as prophylaxis.
keyPoints: Quinolones such as levofloxacin or ciprofloxacin are used in combination with amoxicillin-clavulanate for outpatient therapy.
This antibiotic regimen is suitable only if the patient has not been on quinolone prophylaxis.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · OUTPATIENT THERAPY FOR NEUTROPENIC FEVER

items.title: Recognizing High-Risk Bacteremia in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: Neutropenic patients with fever and bacteremia, particularly from gram-negative bacteria, have significantly higher mortality rates. It's crucial to identify whether the source is mucosal barrier injury or a central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI).
keyPoints: Bacteremia occurs in 10% of neutropenic febrile patients.
Gram-negative bacteremia carries higher mortality than gram-positive.
Mucosal barrier injury from chemotherapy/radiation is a common cause.
Differentiate between CLABSI and MBI based on the type of organism isolated.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · BACTEREMIA

items.title: Diagnosing Catheter-Related Bacteremia
items.content: When diagnosing catheter-related bacteremia, repeat blood cultures from both central and peripheral sites. A differential time to positivity of at least 2 hours favors a diagnosis of catheter-related infection.
keyPoints: Repeat blood cultures if gram-positive organisms are found.
Confirm bacteremia with positive cultures from both central and peripheral sites.
Differential time to positivity >2h indicates catheter-related bacteremia.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · BACTEREMIA

items.title: Management of CLABSI in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: The preferred treatment for CLABSI is catheter removal, though this may not be feasible in thrombocytopenic patients. Antibiotic lock therapy can be considered, and a 48-hour line holiday is customary before replacing the catheter.
keyPoints: Preferred treatment for CLABSI is catheter removal.
Consider antibiotic lock therapy if feasible.
A 48-hour 'line holiday' is typical before re-catheterization.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · BACTEREMIA

items.title: Management of Sinusitis in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: In neutropenic patients, sinusitis may be caused by uncommon pathogens and can progress to invasive fungal infections. Evaluation with CT and otolaryngology consultation is recommended for culture and mucosal integrity assessment. Invasive fungal sinusitis necessitates surgical resection alongside antifungal therapy.
keyPoints: Sinusitis in neutropenic patients may involve S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and other gram-negative bacilli.
CT findings of fungal sinusitis include marked asymmetry, heterogeneous radiodensity, and bony erosions.
Empirical antifungal therapy often includes a lipid formulation of amphotericin B and posaconazole.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Pneumonia in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: Pulmonary infiltrates in immunocompromised patients require a systematic differential diagnosis approach, considering both infectious and noninfectious causes. Early bronchoscopy with BAL is recommended for neutropenic patients with pneumonia.
keyPoints: Noninfectious conditions to consider include heart failure, fluid overload, TACO, TRALI, and chemotherapy-associated pneumonitis.
Radiologic appearance helps differentiate between bacterial/mold infections (unilateral focal) and viral/atypical bacteria/PJP (multifocal/diffuse).
Empirical antimicrobial regimen should cover antipseudomonal $\beta$-lactams, vancomycin, and an agent active against Legionella.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PJP) in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: In neutropenic patients with additional risk factors like corticosteroid use, empirical coverage for PJP is reasonable. PCR of respiratory secretions is the gold standard for diagnosis, while serum $\beta$-D-glucan can support but not confirm PJP.
keyPoints: Empirical TMP-SMX for PJP depends on risk factors; neutropenia alone suggests low likelihood.
PCR is the gold standard for PJP diagnosis; serum $\beta$-D-glucan supports diagnosis but is not specific.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Empirical Antifungal Therapy in High-Risk Patients
items.content: In patients at high risk for invasive fungal disease (IFD), empirical antifungal therapy is often initiated before a definitive diagnosis. The choice of antifungal depends on the prophylactic regimen already in use.
keyPoints: Initiation or escalation of antifungal therapy is common prior to IFD diagnosis in high-risk patients.
If no antifungal prophylaxis or fluconazole is used, a mold-active azole (e.g., voriconazole) is reasonable pending diagnosis due to the likelihood of Aspergillus infection.
Switching to liposomal amphotericin B is recommended if IFD is suspected while on a mold-active azole.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 2) | INTRA-ABDOMINAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Evaluation of Suspected Pneumonia
items.content: Evaluation involves multiple diagnostic tests, and further invasive procedures may be necessary if initial studies are negative or the patient does not improve.
keyPoints: Sputum and blood cultures, nasopharyngeal swab for PCR, urine Legionella antigen, serum galactomannan, beta-glucan are part of the evaluation.
If empirical antimicrobial regimen fails, consider early bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) or image-guided percutaneous lung biopsy.
Consider broad differential diagnosis and repeat imaging if pneumonia worsens despite initial diagnosis.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 2) | INTRA-ABDOMINAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Management of Neutropenic Enterocolitis (Typhlitis)
items.content: Neutropenic enterocolitis requires conservative management with broad-spectrum antibiotics, and surgery may be needed if complications arise.
keyPoints: Clinical syndrome includes fever, abdominal pain, tenderness, and diarrhea; CT scan shows right lower quadrant inflammatory mass.
Conservative treatment involves broad-spectrum antibiotics like carbapenems or piperacillin-tazobactam.
Surgery may be necessary for complications such as intestinal perforation or peritonitis.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (part 2) | INTRA-ABDOMINAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Bacterial CNS Infections in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: In neutropenic patients, bacterial CNS infections are uncommon and often secondary to bacteremia or paranasal sinus extension. Gram-negative bacilli like Pseudomonas aeruginosa can cause meningitis. Rarely, Rothia mucilaginosa and Bacillus cereus may lead to difficult-to-treat meningitis or brain abscesses.
keyPoints: Bacterial CNS infections are uncommon during neutropenia.
Common causes include gram-negative bacilli like Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
Rothia mucilaginosa and Bacillus cereus can cause rare, difficult-to-treat meningitis or brain abscesses.
Successful treatment often involves meropenem and vancomycin.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS

items.title: Listeria monocytogenes in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Listeria monocytogenes causes meningitis, rhombencephalitis, and brain abscesses primarily in patients with impaired cellular immunity. The recommended treatment is ampicillin plus gentamicin, or TMP-SMX for penicillin allergy.
keyPoints: Common in immunocompromised patients, including those on immunosuppressive regimens.
Can cause meningitis, rhombencephalitis, and brain abscesses.
Recommended treatment: ampicillin plus gentamicin or TMP-SMX for penicillin allergy.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS

items.title: Opportunistic Pathogens in Prolonged Neutropenia
items.content: In patients with prolonged neutropenia, brain lesions may indicate opportunistic fungal infections. Aspergillus and Candida cause hematogenous abscesses; mucormycosis often extends from the sinuses. Empirical treatment includes meropenem, vancomycin, and antifungals like voriconazole or liposomal amphotericin B.
keyPoints: Brain lesions in prolonged neutropenia suggest opportunistic fungal infections.
Aspergillus and Candida cause hematogenous abscesses; mucormycosis often extends from sinuses.
Empirical treatment: meropenem, vancomycin, voriconazole for CNS aspergillosis, liposomal amphotericin B for mucormycosis.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM INFECTIONS

items.title: Biopsy Focal Skin Lesions in Neutropenic, Febrile Patients
items.content: Focal skin lesions in neutropenic, febrile patients should be biopsied due to the multiple potential causes, some of which may be life-threatening and require immediate treatment.
keyPoints: Biopsy is recommended for focal skin lesions in neutropenic, febrile patients.
Causes can be diverse and potentially life-threatening.
Immediate treatment may be necessary based on biopsy results.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS

items.title: Empiric Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Coverage for Cellulitis in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: In neutropenic patients with cellulitis, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic coverage should include an antipseudomonal β-lactam and vancomycin until the etiologic diagnosis is established.
keyPoints: Cellulitis may be caused by streptococci, S. aureus, or gram-negative bacilli.
Empiric treatment includes antipseudomonal β-lactam and vancomycin.
Broad-spectrum coverage is necessary until diagnosis is confirmed.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS | C | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | ENVIRONMENTAL PRECAUTIONS
items.title: Management of Urinary Tract Infections in Neutropenic Fever (NF) Patients
items.content: In patients with neutropenic fever, urine cultures often reveal bacteriuria.
