It can be challenging to distinguish between asymptomatic bacteriuria and a true urinary tract infection that requires treatment. Empirical antibiotic regimens for NF typically cover most urinary pathogens, making it appropriate to treat bacteriuria in these cases. If UTI is suspected as the source of NF, CT imaging should be considered.
keyPoints: Urine cultures are common in patients with neutropenic fever (NF).
Bacteriuria may be asymptomatic or indicate a true UTI needing treatment.
Empirical antibiotic regimens for NF cover most urinary pathogens.
CT imaging is recommended if UTI is suspected as the source of NF.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS | C | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | ENVIRONMENTAL PRECAUTIONS

items.subtopic: URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS | C | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | ENVIRONMENTAL PRECAUTIONS
items.title: Environmental Precautions to Prevent Infections in High-Risk Cancer Patients
items.content: Inpatient leukemia and transplant wards use HEPA filters, appropriate air exchanges, and positive pressure rooms to reduce mold spore exposure and limit airborne transmission of infections. Outpatients receiving chemotherapy should avoid construction sites and gardening activities during periods of immune compromise.
keyPoints: HEPA filters and positive pressure rooms are used in leukemia and transplant wards.
Environmental precautions aim to reduce mold spore exposure, particularly to prevent aspergillosis.
Outpatient settings cannot implement stringent environmental controls like inpatients.
Patients should avoid construction sites and gardening during immune compromise.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS | C | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN PATIENTS WITH CANCER | ENVIRONMENTAL PRECAUTIONS

items.title: Guidelines for Food Safety in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Neutropenic patients should not follow low-microbial diets due to lack of benefit and negative impact on nutrition and quality of life. Instead, they should adhere to CDC guidelines focusing on hand hygiene, proper food handling, avoiding raw or undercooked foods, and being cautious with water sources.
keyPoints: Low-microbial diets show no convincing benefit in well-designed studies for neutropenic patients.
CDC guidelines emphasize: hand hygiene, washing vegetables/fruit, avoiding raw/unpasteurized foods, and awareness of food-borne outbreaks.
Avoid travel to regions with unreliable food/water safety.
Prevent exposure to farm or wild animals; avoid new pets while immunocompromised.
Existing pets should not expose patients directly to animal waste.
Avoid contact with exotic pets like reptiles due to risk of unusual pathogens (e.g., Salmonella).
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS | RESPIRATORY VIRAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Preventing Respiratory Viral Infections in Cancer Patients
items.content: Cancer patients should minimize contact with individuals showing respiratory symptoms and avoid crowded indoor settings. They should be aware of local viral patterns, use additional precautions during outbreaks, and consider masking in high community spread situations.
keyPoints: Avoid contact with persons exhibiting respiratory infection symptoms.
Risk is higher in indoor congregate settings; prefer outdoor events or small gatherings.
Be informed about local viral infection trends (e.g., COVID-19, influenza, RSV).
Use additional precautions during local outbreaks, such as avoiding crowds and masking.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · PROTECTION FROM FOOD-BORNE INFECTIONS | RESPIRATORY VIRAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Guidelines for Sexual Intimacy in Neutropenic Patients with Cancer
items.content: Patients undergoing cancer treatment, particularly those experiencing neutropenia, should avoid sexual intercourse and certain types of stimulation to prevent injury and subsequent infections. Once their Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC) recovers, they can typically resume normal sexual activities.
keyPoints: Sexual activity is advised against during neutropenia due to increased risk of infection from potential injuries.
Resumption of sexual activity is generally safe once ANC has recovered.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · SEXUAL INTIMACY | ANTIMICROBIAL PROPHYLAXIS

items.title: Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Cancer Patients with Neutropenia
items.content: For cancer patients, particularly those with prolonged neutropenia (ANC <500/μL for at least 7 days), prophylactic treatment with a quinolone is recommended. Additionally, acyclovir can be used to prevent infections from HSV and VZV.
keyPoints: Quinolone prophylaxis should be considered in adults with prolonged neutropenia (ANC <500/μL for at least 7 days).
Acyclovir is recommended for preventing HSV and VZV infections.
items.reference: Infections in Patients with Cancer · SEXUAL INTIMACY | ANTIMICROBIAL PROPHYLAXIS
